methodology_sections: 2.3 Data Flow Tracing - Input flow, external data, asset movement, permission escalation
3.2 Business Logic Analysis - Economic incentives, edge cases, assumption violations, race conditions

transformations: Share ratio calculation via min(baseInputRatio, quoteInputRatio)
Dead shares burn (1001 on first mint)
data_flows.trust_changes: User token balance transitions to pool-managed reserves; trust in pool accounting
exposure_risks: Front-running first deposit to manipulate share price
Excess tokens sent beyond ratio are retained by pool but not credited proportionally
manipulation_risks: First depositor can manipulate I parameter ratio to steal from subsequent depositors
Donation attack: send tokens directly to pool before buyShares to skew ratios

data_flows.source: Trader swap input (base or quote token)
transformations: PMM pricing calculation with quadratic formula
data_flows.trust_changes: tx.origin used for fee rate lookup instead of msg.sender - trust bypassed for meta-transactions
exposure_risks: tx.origin usage in sellBase/sellQuote allows fee manipulation via intermediary contracts
manipulation_risks: tx.origin for fee rate: If FeeRateModel gives different rates based on trader address, using tx.origin means a contract calling on behalf of a user gets the user's fee rate, not its own
Sandwich attacks on swaps with no slippage protection at the MagicLP level

transformations: Post-callback balance validation against PMM pricing
data_flows.trust_changes: Untrusted callback to assetTo contract; reentrancy guarded by nonReentrant
validation_points: PMM price check for swap-equivalent validation
exposure_risks: Flash loan can be used for price manipulation in external protocols
manipulation_risks: Flash loan with both base and quote outputs: if baseBalance >= _BASE_RESERVE_ and quoteBalance >= _QUOTE_RESERVE_, no swap validation occurs - free flash loan without fees
The mtFee transfer in flashLoan reduces balance further before _sync, but _sync reads balanceOf which already accounts for the transfer

transformations: Target reduction using divCeil (rounds up reduction, favoring protocol)
data_flows.trust_changes: Callback to `to` address after token transfers - potential for reentrancy but guarded
exposure_risks: Target values reduced with divCeil may cause slight value leakage from LPs over many operations
manipulation_risks: If there are tokens sent directly to the pool (donations), sellShares gives proportional claim to actual balance, not just reserves - this can be gamed

transformations: If locked: amount * lockingBoostMultiplerInBips / BIPS for effective balance
Lock duration aligned to next epoch + lockDuration
data_flows.trust_changes: Operator can stakeFor any account - trust in operator
exposure_risks: Operator can force-stake for arbitrary accounts, potentially griefing by locking small amounts
manipulation_risks: Rewards distributed proportionally to boosted balance; a user can lock/unlock timing to maximize reward capture at epoch boundaries

transformations: Remaining rewards from current period added to new amount
data_flows.trust_changes: Operator trusted to provide correct reward amounts matching transferred tokens
exposure_risks: Reward rate truncation: amount / _remainingRewardTime loses precision, dust remains in contract
manipulation_risks: Calling notifyRewardAmount just before epoch ends (small _remainingRewardTime) inflates rewardRate for a very short period, then rewards reset next epoch - potential for reward timing manipulation

transformations: If lock expired: transfer previous locked amount, store new amount
If lock not expired: add to existing lock
sinks: New rewards locked until next epoch
data_flows.trust_changes: Reward tokens only claimable one epoch after earning
exposure_risks: If reward token is removed or changed, locked items may reference stale indices
manipulation_risks: User can time getRewards calls to just after epoch boundary to immediately claim locked rewards from previous period while locking new ones for only a short time

transformations: Target and reserve reset to current balances
data_flows.trust_changes: Implementation owner has full control over pool parameters
exposure_risks: Owner can drain pool by setting large baseOutAmount/quoteOutAmount within reserve limits
manipulation_risks: Truncation bugs: _LP_FEE_RATE_ stored as uint64 but validated up to 1e16 which fits; _I_ stored as uint128 but validated up to 10^36 which fits in uint128 (max ~3.4e38). However _K_ is stored as uint64 but validated up to 10^18 which does NOT fit in uint64 (max ~1.84e19). Wait, 10^18 = 1e18 and uint64 max = 18446744073709551615 ≈ 1.84e19, so 1e18 fits. OK both fit.

path: Each hop: sellBase/sellQuote sending output to next LP
transformations: directions bitmask determines sell base vs sell quote at each hop
Each intermediate result auto-deposited in next pool
data_flows.trust_changes: Router trusts path[] addresses as valid MagicLP pools - no validation
validation_points: minimumOut slippage check on final output only
exposure_risks: Malicious LP addresses in path can steal intermediate tokens
No validation that path[i] is actually a registered MagicLP pool
manipulation_risks: Intermediate hops have no slippage protection; only final output is checked against minimumOut

transformations: Salt computed from tx.origin, implementation, tokens, params
data_flows.trust_changes: tx.origin used as creator - same issue as swaps; contracts cannot be attributed correctly
validation_points: Init validates addresses, I, K, lpFeeRate ranges
exposure_risks: Anyone can create pools for any token pair
tx.origin as creator means the actual caller contract is not tracked
manipulation_risks: Fake pools can be created with adversarial FeeRateModel if factory is called directly (but factory uses its own maintainerFeeRateModel)

core_rules: MagicLP uses PMM (Proactive Market Maker) algorithm with constant I (price) parameter instead of oracle
LP shares minted proportionally to min(baseInputRatio, quoteInputRatio) * totalSupply
First LP deposit burns 1001 shares to address(0) as dead shares to prevent inflation attacks
All minted amounts must exceed 1000 to prevent dust griefing
LockingMultiRewards rewards are delayed by one epoch (locked until next epoch boundary)
Locked staking gets a boost multiplier (lockingBoostMultiplerInBips / BIPS) applied to effective balance
Fee rates split between LP fee (stays in pool) and maintainer fee (extracted)
Pool reserves tracked as uint112 with overflow protection
economic_incentives: Trading fees: lpFeeRate stays in pool (benefits LPs), mtFeeRate goes to maintainer
Locking boost: locked tokens count as (amount * lockingBoostMultiplerInBips / BIPS) for reward calculation
Lock duration: 13 weeks by default, locks expire at epoch-aligned boundaries
Reward distribution: rewards distributed per-second over rewardsDuration epochs
governance_mechanisms: MagicLP: onlyImplementationOwner can change parameters, rescue tokens
Factory: owner can set implementation, add/remove pools, set fee rate model
LockingMultiRewards: owner manages reward tokens, pausing; operators distribute rewards and process locks
FeeRateModel: owner sets maintainer and implementation

edge_cases.scenario: buyShares with baseReserve > 0 but quoteReserve == 0 (or vice versa)
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Falls through both if-branches (totalSupply==0 and baseReserve>0&&quoteReserve>0) without minting any shares. shares remains 0, then _mint(to, 0) reverts with ErrMintAmountNotEnough since 0 <= 1000
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: An attacker who gets the pool into a state with one reserve at 0 but totalSupply > 0 effectively bricks the pool for new deposits. This could happen via sellShares draining one side through rounding, then syncing.

edge_cases.scenario: flashLoan with both baseBalance >= _BASE_RESERVE_ and quoteBalance >= _QUOTE_RESERVE_
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Should charge some fee for the flash loan
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Neither the sell-quote nor sell-base branch executes; tokens are returned without fees. The pool just syncs the new (higher) balances. This is essentially a free flash loan.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Attacker can borrow tokens for free within a single transaction if they return equal or greater amounts of both tokens. While they must return the tokens, the zero-fee flash loan can be used for arbitrage or governance manipulation.

edge_cases.scenario: sellBase/sellQuote using tx.origin for fee rate lookup
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Fee rate should be determined by the actual entity performing the swap
edge_cases.actual_behavior: tx.origin is used, meaning contracts calling on behalf of users get the user's fee rate. If FeeRateModel has per-address fee rates, this can be manipulated.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: A privileged tx.origin (e.g., whitelisted for lower fees) could be tricked into signing a transaction that routes through a malicious contract performing swaps at reduced fees.

edge_cases.scenario: setParameters truncates _LP_FEE_RATE_ to uint64, _K_ to uint64, _I_ to uint128
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Values should be stored exactly as validated
edge_cases.actual_behavior: The validation checks newLpFeeRate against MAX_LP_FEE_RATE (1e16, fits uint64), newK against MAX_K (1e18, fits uint64 since uint64 max ≈ 1.84e19), newI against MAX_I (1e36, fits uint128). These all fit, so no truncation issue. However, init() stores values without truncation (using uint256 state vars), while setParameters casts. This inconsistency is suspicious but not exploitable given current ranges.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: None with current parameter bounds, but if MAX_I or MAX_K were increased, truncation could silently reduce values.

edge_cases.scenario: LockingMultiRewards: processExpiredLocks reorders locks via swap-and-pop
edge_cases.expected_behavior: lastLockIndex should correctly track the user's most recent lock after reordering
edge_cases.actual_behavior: If lastLockIndex[user] == lastIndex (last element), it's updated to `index`. But if the lock being processed IS the lastLockIndex and locks.length > 1, it reverts with ErrInvalidLockIndex. This prevents processing the most recent lock unless it's the only lock remaining.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Operator must process locks in a specific order. The last lock index cannot be processed until it's the only remaining lock. This could delay unlocking for users with multiple locks if operator doesn't process in correct order.

edge_cases.scenario: LockingMultiRewards: reward token added after users have existing reward locks
edge_cases.expected_behavior: New reward token should integrate smoothly with existing reward lock mechanism
edge_cases.actual_behavior: In _getRewards, if i >= rewardItemLength (new token index exceeds existing lock items), it pushes a new RewardLockItem. This works for the expired case, but the newly pushed item's previous locked amount is 0 (correct). However, if the lock is NOT expired, the new item is pushed with just the current rewardAmount, which is correct. No issue here.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Minimal - the logic handles this correctly by checking index bounds.

edge_cases.actual_behavior: Division by zero in `_BASE_TARGET_ = uint112((uint256(_BASE_TARGET_) * baseBalance) / uint256(_BASE_RESERVE_))` if _BASE_RESERVE_ is 0 but baseBalance changed. This would revert.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Not directly exploitable since it reverts, but could prevent ratioSync from working if reserves somehow reach 0.

edge_cases.expected_behavior: Should handle case where base has more decimals than quote
edge_cases.actual_behavior: The check `quoteDecimals - baseDecimals > MAX_BASE_QUOTE_DECIMALS_DIFFERENCE` uses unsigned subtraction. If quoteDecimals < baseDecimals, this underflows and wraps to a very large number, always reverting. This means base decimals can NEVER exceed quote decimals.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: This is a significant bug. Any token pair where the base token has more decimals than the quote token cannot be created through the Router. For example, a base token with 18 decimals and a quote token with 6 decimals would be rejected, even though this is a common and valid configuration (e.g., ETH/USDC). Users must use Factory.create directly to bypass this restriction.

edge_cases.actual_behavior: If selling all shares except the 1001 dead shares, baseAmount and quoteAmount are calculated proportionally. The dead shares permanently lock a small fraction of tokens in the pool.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Minimal - dead shares are by design. Very small dust remains locked.

edge_cases.scenario: Flash loan combined swap - mtFee deducted from contract balance before _sync
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Reserves should accurately reflect post-fee balances
edge_cases.actual_behavior: In flashLoan, when a sell-base case is detected, mtFee is transferred out via _transferQuoteOut, then _sync reads the post-transfer balance. This is correct. But the actual swap output check compares `_QUOTE_RESERVE_ - quoteBalance > receiveQuoteAmount`, checking if the effective output exceeds the PMM-calculated output. However, mtFee was already sent to the maintainer BEFORE _sync, so the final quoteBalance read by _sync is reduced by mtFee. This means LPs effectively absorb the mtFee cost in the flash loan case.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: In flash loans, the maintainer fee is paid from the pool's existing balance rather than from the borrower's repayment, effectively taxing LPs. Over many flash loan operations, LP value is eroded.

edge_cases.scenario: LockingMultiRewards: _getRewards assumes rewardTokens ordering matches RewardLockItem ordering
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Items are pushed in order of rewardTokens array. If rewardTokens order is guaranteed immutable (push only), this holds. Since rewardTokens only grows (addReward pushes, no removal), the ordering is preserved. However, if a token at index i is claimed when expired, the item.amount is overwritten with the new rewardAmount for the SAME index. The item.token field is only set on push, never updated. So if somehow ordering diverged, wrong tokens would be transferred.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Since addReward only appends, ordering is preserved. Low risk.

edge_cases.scenario: Reward calculation precision loss in LockingMultiRewards
edge_cases.expected_behavior: All distributed rewards should be claimable
edge_cases.actual_behavior: rewardRate = amount / _remainingRewardTime truncates. Over the period, actual distributed rewards = rewardRate * duration < amount. The difference (dust) remains in the contract permanently. Additionally, pendingRewardsPerToken = (timeElapsed * rewardRate * 1e18) / totalSupply_ truncates, losing precision per user.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Dust accumulation over time. Not directly exploitable but represents value leakage from reward distributors.

assumption_violations: Router._validateDecimals assumes quoteDecimals >= baseDecimals due to unsigned subtraction - this is not always true for valid token pairs
MagicLP assumes _BASE_TOKEN_ and _QUOTE_TOKEN_ are standard ERC20 tokens without fee-on-transfer, rebasing, or other non-standard behavior
Factory.create uses tx.origin as creator - assumes direct EOA interaction, breaks for smart contract wallets and meta-transactions
sellBase/sellQuote use tx.origin for fee rate - assumes caller is EOA, breaks fee model for contract-based traders
LockingMultiRewards assumes reward tokens never change behavior (no fee-on-transfer consideration on safeTransferFrom for notifyRewardAmount - actual received may be less than amount)
flashLoan assumes atomic consistency but mtFee extraction from pool balance between swap validation and _sync can introduce accounting discrepancy
game_theory_risks: Sandwich attacks on MagicLP swaps: MagicLP has no built-in slippage protection; relies on Router for minimumOut

LockingMultiRewards: Users can stake just before reward notification and unstake just after to capture disproportionate rewards. The epoch-based lock mitigates but doesn't eliminate this for unlocked staking.
First depositor attack: First LP can deposit minimal amounts to set unfavorable share ratios, though dead shares (1001) mitigate this partially
MEV extraction: Operator calling notifyRewardAmount can be front-run by users who stake just before, then unstake after the first reward update
Lock manipulation: Users can time locks to start just before epoch boundary, getting full lockDuration credit for minimal additional lock time
integration_risks: MagicLP as collateral in Cauldron: MagicLpAggregator only safe for closely-priced pairs; misuse with divergent pairs causes mispricing and potential bad debt
Fee-on-transfer tokens: Neither MagicLP nor LockingMultiRewards account for tokens that take fees on transfer, leading to accounting mismatches
Rebasing tokens: If base or quote token is rebasing, balance changes without transfers break reserve tracking
External FeeRateModel: Malicious or buggy FeeRateImpl can return excessive fee rates, potentially draining swap outputs to maintainer
WETH wrapping in Router: If Router receives ETH but WETH wrapping fails, ETH could be stuck (mitigated by receive() fallback but no explicit recovery)
calculation_errors: Math._SolveQuadraticFunctionForTrade: The part2 calculation `(((k * V0) / V1) * V0) + (i * delta)` computes kQ0^2/Q1 but then ADDS i*delta. The comment says 'kQ0^2/Q1-i*deltaB' but since deltaBSig is always negative (selling), the formula adds rather than subtracts. This matches the mathematical derivation where the negative delta is already accounted for by treating it as positive input.
DecimalMath.divFloor potential overflow: (target * ONE) can overflow for large target values when target > type(uint256).max / 1e18

getMidPrice in PMMPricing: When state.R == RState.ONE (R=1 case), the BELOW_ONE branch is entered (since RState.ONE == 0 which is not == BELOW_ONE). Actually, RState.ONE = 0, so the if-check `state.R == RState.BELOW_ONE` (which is 2) is false, and `state.R == RState.ABOVE_ONE` (which is 1) is also false for R=ONE, so the else branch (ABOVE_ONE formula) is used. This is correct as when R=1, B0=B, so (B0*B0/B)/B = 1, and R = (1-K) + K*1 = 1, and midPrice = i*1 = i. Correct.

token_semantics.notes: Decimals match base token decimals. ERC20 via solady. Minimum mint of 1001. Dead shares of 1001 burned to address(0) on first deposit.
Standard WETH with deposit/withdraw. Used as ETH wrapper in Router.
Assumed standard ERC20. Fee-on-transfer tokens would break stakingTokenBalance accounting. No actual balance check after transfer.
Up to 5 reward tokens. Fee-on-transfer tokens in notifyRewardAmount would cause rewardRate to be inflated relative to actual balance, leading to insolvency.
Arbitrary ERC20. Fee-on-transfer or rebasing tokens break reserve accounting. getBaseInput() relies on balanceOf delta matching actual transfer amount.
Arbitrary ERC20. Same concerns as base token. Router validates decimals difference but with bug (unsigned underflow).

invariants.name: reserve_balance_consistency
invariants.description: After _sync or _setReserve, reserves should equal actual token balances. Violated if fee-on-transfer tokens are used.

invariants.name: target_reserve_ordering
invariants.description: When R=BELOW_ONE: B >= B0 and Q <= Q0. When R=ABOVE_ONE: B <= B0 and Q >= Q0. When R=ONE: B == B0 and Q == Q0.

invariants.name: supply_conservation_locking
invariants.description: unlockedSupply == sum of all users' unlocked balances. lockedSupply == sum of all users' locked balances.

invariants.name: staking_token_solvency
invariants.description: stakingTokenBalance <= stakingToken.balanceOf(address(this)). Difference is skimmable excess.

invariants.name: dead_shares_lock
invariants.description: For any initialized MagicLP pool, balanceOf(address(0)) == 1001, ensuring totalSupply never reaches 0 after first deposit.
